hook: You already know how to integrate a function over an interval to find areas and accumulate quantities in one dimension. But what if your problem lives on a surface, inside a solid, or across a region with curved boundaries? This unit hands you the tools to compute exactly that — turning two- and three-dimensional problems into something you can actually solve.
concept: The unifying idea is that integration can be extended beyond a single variable by slicing a multi-dimensional region into infinitely thin pieces, integrating one variable at a time, and reassembling the result — a process captured by the iterated integral $\int\!\int_R f(x,y)\,dA$ and its three-dimensional counterpart.

topic_roadmap.description: You will learn to evaluate double integrals over rectangular regions by setting up and computing iterated integrals using Fubini's Theorem.
Master the technique of describing non-rectangular regions with variable limits of integration so you can integrate over any bounded, well-defined planar shape.
Apply the polar coordinate substitution $x = r\cos\theta$, $y = r\sin\theta$ to dramatically simplify double integrals over circular or symmetric regions.

topic_roadmap.topic_name: Applications: Area, Volume, Mass, and Center of Mass
topic_roadmap.description: Use double integrals to compute real-world quantities such as the area of a region, the volume beneath a surface, and the center of mass of a lamina with variable density.
Extend your integration skills into three dimensions by evaluating triple integrals over solid regions using carefully ordered iterated integrals in Cartesian coordinates.

topic_roadmap.topic_name: Triple Integrals in Cylindrical and Spherical Coordinates
topic_roadmap.description: Apply cylindrical coordinates $(r, \theta, z)$ and spherical coordinates $(\rho, \theta, \phi)$ to evaluate triple integrals over solids with circular, cylindrical, or spherical symmetry far more efficiently.

topic_roadmap.topic_name: Change of Variables and the Jacobian (H)
topic_roadmap.description: Generalize coordinate substitution to any transformation by computing the Jacobian determinant, giving you a powerful, unified method for simplifying integrals over complex regions.
